figures.caption: Table 1: Performance comparison for generation on the ImageNet dataset at 256× 256 resolution.

Figure 2: Image generation results of DiT-XL and the proposed method based on the recursive sparse reasoning mechanism. Using the recursive method provides richer object textures and finer background details in the generated images.

Figure 4: Adaptive neural module activation conditioned on the current vision tokens, the diffusion timestep, and the class label. Ablating the conditioning to only the current vision tokens results in modules that struggle to diversify across latent steps. Therefore, the optimal choice incorporates both the current states and the additional conditional information.

Figure 3: Latent trajectories of vision tokens during five-step recursion (12th layer). Visualized via PCA compressing all tokens from a single image. Trajectory changes across diffusion timesteps: at early steps (high noise), trajectories are largely coherent, indicating uniform processing; at later steps (low noise), they exhibit divergent, patch-specific pathways, reflecting the neural modules’ context-adaptive specialization.

Figure 1: Architecture of the proposed recursive sparse reasoning mechanism integrated with SD3. A recursive modular component is introduced into the joint attention of vision and text branches. Visual token probabilities are computed across samples, and tokens are reassembled into original spatial order after passing through selected neural modules. At each latent step, outputs of the neural modules correlate with the text branch to progressively refine the representation using text tokens. This results in better-aligned visual generation with respect to the input prompt.

Figure 5: Qualitative comparison of image generations from our recursive approach versus the SD3medium baseline at 1024 × 1024 resolution. Our method achieves higher text alignment and fidelity, whereas SD3 fails to accurately reflect fine-grained prompt details.
Table 2: Evaluation results on the GenEval benchmark.
